data.title: HCA Healthcare Non-GAAP EPS of $6.96 beats by $1.23, revenue of $19.16B beats by $600M
data.description:

  • Today, the Company is updating its 2025 estimated guidance ranges previously issued on July 25, 2025.

    $6.495 to $6.715 billion

    Adjusted EBITDA

    $15.250 to $15.650 billion

    EPS (diluted)

    $25.50 to $27.00 per diluted share

    $27.00 to $28.00 per diluted share
  • Shares +2% PM.

data.title: Trump-Xi meeting: What to expect
data.description:
President Donald Trump will meet China's Xi Jinping next week in their first face-to-face meeting since Trump took office this year, amid escalating trade tensions.

Trump will head to Asia for a five-day trip this weekend, starting with Malaysia for the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) summit. Next, he'll travel to Japan, where he'll meet its new Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi.

His last stop will be South Korea, where he'll meet Xi on the sidelines of the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC) Summit on Thursday.

Ahead of the summit, a delegation led by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer will meet Chinese negotiators led by Vice Premier He Lifeng in Malaysia to iron out trade differences.

"I think we're going to come out very well and everyone's going to be very happy," Trump told reporters about his upcoming meeting with Xi. Earlier this week, he said tech, rare earths, fentanyl, soybeans, and Taiwan would be on the agenda. 

The U.S. and China struck a trade truce back in May following back-to-back tariff moves. The truce will last till November 10.

But since then, tensions flared up as U.S. expanded its export blacklist, China tightened rare earth export controls, and both sides rolled out higher port fees for ships.

Trump then threatened additional 100% tariffs on Chinese goods, but later dialed back his tone, saying the levy is not sustainable.

"With the recent cooling of rhetoric and President Trump's comments about reaching a 'fantastic deal' with China — and about visiting China in early 2026 — we are leaning toward a positive outcome that at least extends, if not builds on, the uneasy trade truce of the past few months," Lynn Song, ING's chief economist, Greater China.

data.title: IBM's key quantum computing algorithm can work with standard AMD chips: report
data.description:
IBM (NYSE:IBM) said it can run a vital quantum computing algorithm on commonly available chips from Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD), Reuters reported.

Jay Gambetta, the IBM vice president running the firm's quantum efforts, said the work showed that IBM's algorithm not only works in the real world, but can operate on a readily available AMD chip that is not "ridiculously expensive," the report added.

IBM did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

"Implementing it, and showing that the implementation is actually 10 times faster than what is needed, is a big deal," said Gambetta in an interview, according to the report.

Gambetta noted that the algorithm work disclosed on Friday was completed a year ahead of schedule, the report noted.

IBM has a multi-year plan to develop a quantum computer called Starling by 2029.

The company is racing to develop quantum computing against Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet's (GOOGL) (GOOG) Google, which announced a breakthrough algorithm this week.

Alphabet's CEO Sundar Pichai said then that the tech giant's quantum computing chip, known as Willow, has achieved the “first-ever verifiable quantum advantage.”

Quantum computers use qubits to solve problems which can take conventional computers thousands of years to solve —  problems such as how trillions of atoms react over time. However, qubits are prone to errors that can quickly overwhelm the useful computing work of a quantum chip, the report added.

Shares of IBM rose about 1% premarket on Friday.

data.title: Target's 'painful' job cuts are seen by Jefferies as laying the foundation for a turnaround
data.description:
Target Corporation (NYSE:TGT) traded slightly higher after announcing a major corporate downsizing, with the removal of 1,800 jobs. The restructuring is aimed at helping Target (TGT) streamline decision-making, improve profitability, and position the Minneapolis-based company for growth

Jefferies analyst Corey Tarlowe said the job cuts were painful but necessary after years of weak sales for the retailer. Importantly, the move is seen as signaling incoming CEO Michael Fiddelke is willing to act decisively, easing fears of a status quo approach and laying groundwork for a potential turnaround despite near-term headwinds. "We view it as a constructive signal for long-term investors, though evidence of top-line recovery will be key before sentiment improves," advised Tarlowe.

The firm has a Buy rating on Target (TGT) but warned that execution during the leadership transition and persistent demand softness remain near-term headwinds.

Shares of Target (TGT) were up 0.7% in premarket trading. Target (TGT) is lined up to report third-quarter earnings on November 19. The company is expected to report revenue of $25.4 billion and EPS of $1.76.

data.title: Parsons awarded seat on $10B ceiling value U.S. Navy integration and logistics support contract
data.description:
Parsons (NYSE:PSN) Friday said that it was awarded a seat on the Worldwide Expeditionary Multiple Award Contract (WEXMAC), Territorial Integrity of the U.S. (TITUS) 2.1 by the U.S. Naval Supply Systems Command.

This $10 billion ceiling value Indefinite Delivery Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) contract provides critical supplies and services to support U.S. military and coalition partner operations, humanitarian assistance, and disaster relief and consists of a five-year base period and one five-year option period.

Under this contract, Parsons will compete for task orders to support key U.S. Navy operational requirements such as theater opening, sustainment, distribution, stability operations, and civil support, including Defense Support of Civil Authorities.

data.title: Coya Therapeutics slides after pricing $20M stock offering
data.description:
• Coya Therapeutics (NASDAQ:COYA) has priced a public offering of 3,636,364 shares at $5.50 each, aiming to raise approximately $20.0 million.
  • The underwriter has a 30-day option to buy an additional 545,454 shares at the same price, minus fees. The offering is expected to close around October 27, 2025, pending usual closing conditions.
  • The company intends to use the net proceeds from the public offering for working capital and general corporate purposes, including funding its clinical development plan.
  • COYA --12.7% premarket to $6.09
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: TikTok, Meta breached transparency rules under Digital Services Act, EU says
data.description:
The European Commission said Friday it has preliminarily found that both ByteDance-owned (BDNCE) TikTok and Meta (NASDAQ:META) violated the bloc’s transparency requirements under the Digital Services Act ((DSA)).

The Commission preliminarily found both platforms in breach of their obligation to grant researchers adequate access to public data under the act by placing “burdensome procedures and tools to request access to public data.”

It also preliminarily found Meta (NASDAQ:META), for both Instagram and Facebook, in breach of its obligations to provide users simple mechanisms to notify illegal content, as well as to allow them to effectively challenge content moderation decisions.

The findings are part of the commission's formal proceedings launched into Meta (META) and formal proceedings to investigate TikTok under the DSA. These proceedings are distinct from ongoing probes against Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok concerning compliance with other relevant EU law.

“Our democracies depend on trust. That means platforms must empower users, respect their rights, and open their systems to scrutiny. The DSA makes this a duty, not a choice. With today’s actions, we have now issued preliminary findings on researchers’ access to data to four platforms. We are making sure platforms are accountable for their services, as ensured by EU law, towards users and society,” said Henna Virkkunen, executive vice-president for tech sovereignty, security and democracy.
